data.title: Corcept falls as appeals court sides with Teva in Korlym patent dispute
data.description:
Shares of Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) lost more than 20% on Thursday after a federal appeals court ruled against the company in a patent dispute with Israeli drugmaker Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) related to its lead product, Korlym.

In August 2020, Teva (TEVA) received FDA approval to market a generic version of Korlym, a once-daily oral medication, also known as mifepristone, used to treat a rare condition called Cushing’s syndrome.

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal District issued the ruling on Thursday after Corcept (CORT) appealed a ruling from a New Jersey District Court judge, who determined in 2023 that Teva (TEVA) has not infringed upon the company's U.S. patents 10,195,214 and 10,842,800.

“Because we see no clear error in the district court’s fact findings regarding direct infringement, we need not and do not reach the additional finding of specific intent to induce infringement,” the appeals court judges wrote.

data.title: Old National Bancorp to buy back up to $400M shares
data.description:
Old National Bancorp (ONB) on Thursday announced a doubling in the size of its share repurchase program to up to $400M, effective until February 28, 2027.

The new program replaces the prior $200M stock buyback program, set to expire on February 28, 2026.

The new program may see buybacks conducted from time to time on the open market, in privately negotiated transactions, or through accelerated share repurchase programs.

data.title: Laureate projects 11%-12% revenue growth in 2026 while expanding digital and campus investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Laureate Education, Inc. (LAUR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Eilif Serck-Hanssen, President, CEO & Director, reported, "Laureate delivered another strong year of performance in 2025 with sustained revenue growth and expanding margins. Full year revenue reached $1.7 billion, and adjusted EBITDA was $519 million, both exceeding the guidance we provided last October." Management highlighted execution on growth initiatives and productivity, achieving a historical high margin of 30.5% for the year and a net cash position at year-end.
  • The company returned $217 million to shareholders through its stock repurchase program in 2025 and authorized an additional $150 million increase, emphasizing a focus on long-term value creation. Investments included two new campus openings and expansion in Health Sciences with a new medical and veterinary school. Serck-Hanssen emphasized, "We now serve more than 100,000 students in our fully online programs focused on working adults."
  • Laureate’s institutions in Mexico and Peru were recognized for academic excellence, including multiple 5-star ratings by QS Stars and top rankings in national surveys.
  • "For 2026, our guidance called for U.S. dollar reported revenue growth of 11% to 12%, of which approximately 5 points is attributable to the more favorable FX environment," stated CEO Serck-Hanssen. Expansion plans include additional new campuses and further investments in digital education, particularly AI-driven online offerings.
  • Richard Buskirk, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "Revenue in the fourth quarter was $541 million and adjusted EBITDA was $204 million. Both metrics were ahead of the guidance we provided 3 months ago, aided primarily by improved currency rates."

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 total enrollments to be in the range of 516,000 to 521,000 students, and revenue in the range of $1.890 billion to $1.905 billion, reflecting 11% to 12% growth on an as-reported basis and 6% to 7% on an organic constant currency basis. Adjusted EBITDA is forecasted between $583 million and $593 million, with a 50 basis point margin expansion. Adjusted earnings per share are expected between $1.95 and $2.03.
  • Management stated, "We expect improved conditions in the second half of the year and as we head into 2027 following the conclusion of the renegotiated USMCA agreement."
  • Ongoing investments in new campuses and digital capabilities, with continued focus on online segment growth in Peru and Mexico.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $541 million and adjusted EBITDA was $204 million. Fourth quarter net income was $172 million, resulting in earnings per share of $1.17. Adjusted net income was $112 million, and adjusted earnings per share was $0.76, a 46% increase compared to the fourth quarter of the prior year.
  • For the full year, revenue was $1.702 billion and adjusted EBITDA was $519 million with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.5%. Net income for the year was $284 million, with earnings per share of $1.89. Adjusted net income for 2025 was $256 million and adjusted earnings per share was $1.72, up 22% compared to prior year.
  • Mexico's new enrollments increased 5% and Peru’s increased 13% for the year. Margins in Mexico expanded by 164 basis points, and Peru’s revenue growth was 7% with a margin expansion of 54 basis points.
  • Laureate ended the year with $147 million in cash and $129 million in gross debt, for a net cash position of $18 million. The Board authorized a $150 million increase to the share repurchase program, with $181 million available under current authorization as of year-end.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: Asked about campus expansion timing and buy vs. build strategy. Serck-Hanssen responded, "It takes about 18 to 24 months to launch a new campus... Typically, it's more economical for us to just build the campus."
  • Silber further inquired about AI disruption risk. Serck-Hanssen replied, "AI is going to be our friend. AI is going to improve retention. It is going to improve the learning outcomes."
  • Marcelo Santos, JPMorgan: Asked about deceleration in FX-neutral revenue growth and online expansion in Peru. Buskirk attributed deceleration to softer macro conditions in Mexico and capacity constraints in Peru, but highlighted continued margin expansion. Serck-Hanssen noted robust growth in Peru’s online market, with pricing discipline favoring growth.
  • Lucas Nagano, Morgan Stanley: Queried about adjusted EPS guidance assumptions and capacity constraints in Peru. Buskirk cited a small G&A increase and slightly improved taxes. Capacity constraints in Peru are being addressed with new campuses.
  • Eduardo Resende, UBS: Asked about economic softness and tuition pricing. Serck-Hanssen described Mexico's softer conditions as a continuation of 2025, with momentum expected to continue into 2026 and potential upside post-USMCA clarity.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ questions focused on growth drivers, campus expansion timing, AI disruption, and macroeconomic impacts, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive but probing for risks and strategic details.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing resilience and opportunity. Serck-Hanssen and Buskirk consistently provided detailed responses, with statements such as, "We remain well positioned to continue to invest in future growth and innovation while maintaining our commitment to returning excess capital to shareholders."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained similar sentiment levels, but this quarter highlighted more forward-looking confidence regarding digital expansion and capital return.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 projects higher revenue and margin growth than the raised 2025 outlook from the previous quarter, with an explicit focus on digital expansion and new campus launches.
  • Strategic focus has shifted more strongly toward scaling online education and AI-enabled offerings, while campus expansion remains a priority.
  • Analysts’ questions remained focused on growth, margins, and macro impacts, with new attention to AI and digital innovation. Management’s confidence appeared consistent, with greater emphasis on margin expansion and capital return in the current quarter.
  • Key metric changes include higher full-year revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and EPS, as well as increased capital return via share repurchases.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty in Mexico, including the impact of USMCA trade negotiations, as a factor influencing growth expectations. Serck-Hanssen stated, "The softer economic conditions in Mexico is really a continuation of what we have seen since the second half of 2024."
  • Capacity constraints in Peru were acknowledged, with mitigation through new campus launches and expansion of online programs.
  • Analyst concerns centered on pricing discipline, market competition in digital education, and the impact of macroeconomic softness on investments and tuition.

Final Takeaway

Laureate Education finished 2025 with record revenue and margins, outperforming its guidance and returning substantial capital to shareholders. Management’s 2026 outlook points to double-digit revenue growth and continued margin expansion, supported by investments in campus and digital capacity, especially in AI-driven online education. While macroeconomic headwinds persist in Mexico, and capacity constraints exist in Peru, Laureate’s leadership sees sustained opportunity for growth and value creation through disciplined execution, innovation, and an expanded capital return program.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alight signals $100M capital deployment for innovation and operational excellence as new CEO charts turnaround strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alight, Inc. (ALIT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Rohit Verma, CEO, began his tenure by emphasizing Alight's competitive advantages, stating, "Our scale, client relationships, domain expertise and operational footprint provide a significant competitive advantage and leadership position in the marketplace." Verma highlighted Alight's reach, serving "a wide spectrum of employers, including the majority of the Fortune 100" and offering "essential and unmatched benefit solutions via platform that offers extensive flexibility to accommodate a wide range of client needs."
  • He acknowledged underperformance in 2025, remarking, "In 2025, we did not meet our internal financial targets and new bookings and renewals did not meet our expectations, leading us to miss our forecast to the market."
  • The CEO outlined immediate priorities: "driving service and operational excellence," "innovating products enabled by AI to create a cutting-edge user experience," and "building relationships that result in enduring trusted partnerships."
  • Verma reported that in Q4, Alight piloted conversational AI with two large clients, achieving a "significant reduction in channel jumping... indicative of the improved efficiency and participant efficacy experienced with the conversational AI product."
  • On 2025 performance, Verma stated, "We generated $2.3 billion in revenue with adjusted EBITDA of $561 million and an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 25%." He further noted, "the business generated $250 million of free cash flow in 2025, which enabled us to maintain a strong liquidity position."
  • Gregory Giometti, Interim CFO, said, "Revenue for the fourth quarter was $653 million. Recurring revenue of $607 million was down 1.6% compared with the prior year period. Project revenue of $46 million was down 27%. Fourth quarter adjusted gross profit was $272 million, down 9.3% from the prior year period, reflecting an adjusted gross profit margin decline of 240 basis points."
  • Giometti also disclosed a noncash goodwill impairment charge of $803 million for Q4 2025.
  • Verma announced a strategic shift in capital allocation: "We believe it makes sense to take this opportunity to return value to our shareholders through a combination of reducing the company's leverage and through the opportunistic repurchase of stock rather than continuing our quarterly dividend at this time."
  • New leadership appointments were introduced, with Karen Frost leading Health and Navigation and Kevin Curry leading Leaves Solution.

Outlook

  • The CEO refrained from providing full-year guidance, stating, "Given that we missed guidance targets several times in 2025, I don't think it's prudent for me to provide full year guidance when I'm just 30 working days into my role."
  • For Q1 2026, Verma projected, "we expect first quarter 2026 revenue to be down by high single-digit percentage range. Likewise, we anticipate that our planned investments... will create short-term adjusted EBITDA margin pressure, resulting in a decline of 500 to 750 basis points as compared to last year's first quarter."
  • Management highlighted plans to deploy over $100 million of capital to strengthen business foundations and support long-term growth initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 revenue was $653 million, with recurring revenue of $607 million and project revenue of $46 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $178 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 27.3%.
  • Adjusted net income for the quarter was $96 million, with adjusted EPS of $0.18.
  • For the full year, revenue reached $2.3 billion and adjusted EBITDA was $561 million, representing a margin of 24.8%. Adjusted net income was $266 million, with adjusted EPS of $0.50.
  • Free cash flow for the year totaled $250 million. Cash and equivalents at year-end were $273 million, with an undrawn $330 million revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • Peter Christiansen, Citi: Questioned the drivers behind financial underperformance, CEO Verma responded, "the biggest challenge for us has been on driving operational excellence... So the biggest piece that we need to tighten is around execution."
  • Christiansen also asked about measuring milestones, to which Verma replied, "I want to make sure that what I'm giving you is something that is very clear, very definitive and something that I can consistently report on."
  • Scott Schoenhaus, KeyBanc: Asked about Q1 guidance and renewal challenges; Verma explained, "we did not execute well in 2025, specifically on our renewals... our revenue under contract starting 2026 is about 5% down."
  • Schoenhaus further inquired about AI's impact on renewals, with Verma stating, "we have not seen a meaningful or any kind of disruption right now from an AI perspective, neither in terms of the employee base that we have."
  • Peter Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: Sought clarification on the nature of $100 million capital deployment and recurring compensation expenses. Verma said, "the $100 million is not an additional investment. It is the CapEx that we have planned for this year... As far as the nature of the compensation, I do expect that to be recurring."
  • Ross Cole, Needham: Asked about AI-driven margin improvement. Verma indicated, "I don't see any near-term impact on productivity improvement purely from AI... I expect those to be more 2027 opportunities than 2026."
  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: Inquired about renewal slippage, Verma attributed underperformance to "driving operational excellence, being more modern with our user interface, making sure that the relationships that we're building are deep and consistent."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions reflected a slightly negative tone, focusing on financial underperformance, missed renewals, and clarity on milestone measurement, with pressing follow-ups on execution and capital allocation.
  • Management's sentiment during prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, but in Q&A, Verma adopted a cautious tone, repeatedly highlighting the need for improved execution and expressing restraint in offering definitive guidance, as reflected in "I want to make sure that what I'm giving you is something that is very clear, very definitive..."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone is more defensive and less certain, while analysts' skepticism has increased, with concerns shifting more toward execution risk and the impact of leadership transition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from providing specific full-year targets in Q3 to holding back on full-year guidance in Q4, citing recent misses and leadership transition.
  • Strategic focus moved from expanding partner ecosystem and product innovation in Q3 to an urgent emphasis on operational and service execution in Q4.
  • Key metrics declined: Q4 recurring revenue and project revenue both fell compared to the prior year, and adjusted EBITDA margin dropped from the previous year and quarter.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from product rollouts and client renewals in Q3 to capital allocation, dividend termination, and execution risk in Q4.
  • Management's confidence in achieving previous growth and margin targets has softened, with acknowledgment of ongoing challenges and the expectation that 2025's weakness will spill into 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited missed financial targets and lower-than-expected new bookings and renewals for 2025.
  • Increased compensation expense and strategic investments are expected to create near-term margin pressure.
  • Revenue under contract for 2026 is down 5% from the previous year.
  • Noncash goodwill impairment charge of $803 million was recognized in Q4.
  • Termination of the quarterly dividend in favor of share repurchases and debt reduction reflects a shift in capital allocation priorities.
  • Management identified execution, client relationship management, and technology modernization as the most pressing challenges.

Final Takeaway

Alight's new CEO, Rohit Verma, underscored the company's strong client base and platform capabilities but acknowledged that 2025 fell short of expectations due to execution challenges and renewal shortfalls. The company is redirecting $100 million toward operational excellence and innovation, particularly in AI, while discontinuing its quarterly dividend to prioritize share repurchases and debt reduction. Management expects continued headwinds into 2026, with a focus on strengthening execution, rebuilding client trust, and positioning Alight for a future inflection in performance as operational improvements take hold.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: MPT, CVNA, CAR, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower Thursday after the Federal Reserve's latest minutes revealed more hawkish tones than anticipated.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Medical Properties Trust (MPT) +18% - Shares surged after Q4 results topped expectations and concerns tied to former key tenant Prospect Medical Holdings continued to fade. The REIT said it has largely resolved its exposure to Prospect’s restructuring, secured a new 15-year lease for its California hospitals expected to generate ~$45M in annual cash rent by late 2026, and is exiting remaining properties tied to the situation. Q4 revenue came in at $270.3M, above estimates and up sequentially and Y/Y, while cash collections from new tenants improved to $22M, or about 96% of scheduled rent.
  • Etsy (ETSY) +9% - Shares climbed after a modest improvement in GMS and ad revenue helped offset Q4 weaker profits and softer total revenue, while sentiment was also boosted by the announced $1.2B cash sale of its Depop marketplace to eBay. For the current quarter, Etsy expects GMS of $2.38B–$2.43B, up from $2.3B a year ago, with adjusted EBITDA margin projected at 28%–30%, improving from 26.3% last year.
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY) +6% - Shares rose after beating Q4 profit estimates, boosting investor confidence with stronger finances and shareholder returns. The company raised its quarterly dividend by over 8% to $0.26 per share and cut debt by $5.8B following the January 2 completion of its OxyChem sale, bringing total principal debt down to about $15.0B and reinforcing its balance-sheet improvement story.
  • Figma (FIG) +3% - Shares jumped after strong Q4 results and upbeat guidance. Revenue rose 40% Y/Y to $303.8M, topping estimates, while net dollar retention hit 136%, weekly active users surged 70% Q/Q, and customers with over $1M in ARR climbed to 67. The company guided Q1 revenue to $315M–$317M, above the consensus of $292.5M, and FY2026 revenue to $1.36B–$1.37B, above the consensus of $1.29B, implying roughly 38% annual growth, alongside expected non-GAAP operating income of $100M–$110M.
  • DoorDash (DASH) +3% - Shares surged as strong order growth and upbeat guidance outweighed a Q4 top- and bottom-line miss. Orders climbed 32% to 903M, and GOV jumped 39% to $29.7B, both above expectations, while the Q1 outlook for GOV ($31B–$31.8B) and EBITDA ($675M–$775M) came in strong. Investors looked past softer margins and heavy investment spending, focusing on demand momentum and growth visibility.
  • eBay (EBAY) +4% - Shares advanced after delivering a Q4 beat on both revenue and earnings, supported by strong traction from its eBay Live holiday shopping events and ongoing AI-driven initiatives. The company also issued upbeat Q1 guidance, projecting revenue of $3.0B–$3.05B and non-GAAP EPS of $1.53–$1.59, both above consensus estimates. Additionally, eBay approved a new $2B share repurchase program and increased its quarterly cash dividend to $0.31 per share from $0.29.

Biggest stock losers

  • Klarna (KLAR) -26% - Shares tumbled despite strong Q4 results as the company warned GMV growth will slow in Q2 due to tough comps and a gradual rollout of new partnerships. The BNPL firm guided Q1 revenue to $900M–$980M (midpoint below estimates) and adjusted operating profit of $5M–$35M, well short of consensus. GMV is seen at $32B–$33B, and FY GMV is just above $155B, also trailing forecasts. While Klarna highlighted growth drivers like banking products and expanded partnerships with Worldpay, JPMorgan Payments, Stripe, and Nexi, it cautioned merchant activation will take time, weighing on near-term momentum.
  • Avis Budget Group (CAR) -21% - Shares dipped after Q4 results missed expectations, as ongoing repositioning efforts weighed on performance, including another charge tied to reducing the carrying value of its U.S. EV fleet. Management said it is tightening fleet discipline, strengthening the balance sheet, and improving customer experience as it heads into 2026 with a focus on more sustainable earnings growth.
  • Carvana (CVNA) -6% - Shares plunged as investors focused on profitability concerns despite strong Q4 volume and revenue growth. Sales jumped 58% Y/Y to a record $5.6B, and retail units topped expectations, but gross profit per vehicle declined, and wholesale profitability weakened. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $511M but missed estimates, margins narrowed to 9.1%, and the absence of near-term sales guidance added to caution, overshadowing the company’s long-term targets.

data.title: AppLovin moves higher as social media platform job posting gains attention
data.description:
• AppLovin (APP) shares moved higher by 2.5% on Thursday as a job posting discussing building a social media platform has gained attention.
  • The job posting, listed here, advertises for a software engineer to help build the company's “next-generation social platform.”
  • “You'll design and build robust, scalable systems that power everything from seamless media delivery and real-time social interactions to our intelligent content discovery engine,” the posting reads. “Your day might involve optimizing database queries to handle millions of concurrent content requests, designing new APIs for real-time engagement features, and implementing sophisticated services for content ecosystem management.”
  • Bloomberg initially reported on the job posting, notable given that AppLovin failed in trying to acquire the U.S. assets of TikTok.
  • AppLovin did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Appian outlines $50M stock buyback and projects 16% cloud revenue growth for 2026 amid AI-driven momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Appian Corporation (APPN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matthew Calkins highlighted 2025 as a year of strong execution, noting "Appian's cloud subscriptions revenue grew 18% to $117.0 million. Subscriptions revenue grew 19% to $162.3 million. Total revenue grew 22% to $202.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $19.7 million." He emphasized a 50% increase in customers purchasing over $1 million of software, with nearly double the value of 7-figure transactions. Calkins described notable wins, including a European pharmaceutical research organization and a North American aerospace manufacturer, both signing 7-figure deals to leverage Appian's AI capabilities and process modernization.
  • Calkins reported strengthened U.S. public sector positioning, citing a new $500 million, 10-year enterprise agreement with the U.S. Army: "The Army is already an 8-figure ARR customer. This new framework allows it to purchase $500 million in Appian software and services over the next 10 years."
  • He announced a $50 million stock buyback and cited operational improvements: "We created $63 million in operating cash flow compared to a loss of $110 million 2 years ago."
  • Calkins stated, "AI use on our platform grew 14x year-over-year, and we are monetizing that growth. Customers must upgrade to Appian's AI license tier, which comes with an average price increase of 25%." He provided examples of customers upgrading for AI-driven automation and efficiency gains, such as a pharmaceutical company using Appian Doc Center to accelerate response times by 88%.
  • The company welcomed Dave Link to its Board of Directors, citing his expertise in scaling enterprise software companies and AI.
  • CFO Srdjan Tanjga introduced reporting changes and a new metric: "We are introducing a new metric, cloud net ARR expansion... Going forward, we will no longer report cloud gross renewal rate and net revenue retention."
  • Tanjga said, "Appian met or exceeded the guidance ranges we provided on our key metrics of cloud revenue, total revenue and adjusted EBITDA."

Outlook

  • Tanjga guided for Q1 2026 cloud subscription revenue between $119 million and $121 million, and total revenue between $189 million and $193 million. "Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2026 is expected to be between $19 million and $22 million. Non-GAAP earnings per share is expected to be between $0.16 and $0.20."
  • For full year 2026, "cloud subscription revenue is expected to be between $502 million and $510 million, representing year-over-year growth of 16% at the midpoint of the range. Total revenue is expected to be between $801 million and $817 million, representing year-over-year growth of 11% at the midpoint. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to range between $89 million and $99 million for an approximately 12% margin at the midpoint."
  • Guidance assumes professional services growth in the teens for Q1 and high single digits for the year, with FX expected to be a tailwind in Q1 and roughly neutral thereafter.

Financial Results

  • Cloud subscription revenue for Q4 was $117 million, an increase of 18% year-over-year; total subscription revenue reached $162.3 million, up 19%. Total revenue was $202.9 million, up 22% year-over-year.
  • Cloud net ARR expansion was 114% in Q4, up from 112% in Q3. The company reported 140 customers with $1 million plus of ARR, compared to 115 a year ago.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin was 73%; subscription gross profit margin was 86%. Professional services non-GAAP gross margin was 23%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $19.7 million; non-GAAP net income was $11.1 million or $0.15 per diluted share.
  • Cash and investments totaled $187.2 million at year-end. Cash provided by operations for the quarter was $1.1 million, and $62.9 million for the full year.

Q&A

  • Oscar Saavedra, Morgan Stanley, asked about the visibility behind Q1 guidance acceleration. Tanjga replied, "Q1...will benefit from strong new business that we had in Q4, which is why on a sequential basis is a robust guide."
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays, questioned Appian's strategic position as an AI process layer. Calkins responded, "We've been that layer for a long time...enables and governs digital workers."
  • Lenschow also asked about sales capacity. Tanjga said, "We've done a great job significantly improving our sales productivity...this return to growth of the sales org is the beginning of a long-term trend."
  • Steven Enders, Citi, inquired about AI's impact on customer budgets and pipeline. Calkins described AI as "an unalloyed positive for us in our relations with our customers...It's helped our TAM. It's helped our access. It's helped our win rate."
  • Enders asked about the Army agreement. Calkins said, "This is an important moment...It allows us to speak to any part of the organization with great credibility...a wonderful badge of seriousness."
  • James Wood, TD Cowen, asked about Appian's internal AI use and LLM vendor challenges. Calkins stated, "We're using it thoroughly...expecting major increases in all of our development capabilities this year because we're making a prolific use of AI."
  • Wood questioned professional services and on-prem strength. Tanjga explained, "Customers want to get the value but they're sensitive to get it at the levels of accuracy and performance...our success there and the change in how the government likes to deal with vendors has helped our professional services business."
  • Devin Au, KeyBanc, sought clarity on 2026 guidance conservatism. Tanjga replied, "There's no incremental conservatism or caution...cloud, it's just ratable. It's frankly a little bit easier to forecast."
  • Lucky Schreiner, D.A. Davidson, asked about deceleration in cloud guidance. Tanjga attributed it mainly to FX normalization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was positive, emphasizing strong results and future growth, with repeated congratulations on the quarter. They raised questions about guidance visibility, AI opportunity, and sales investment, reflecting interest rather than skepticism.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and assertive, particularly during prepared remarks. Calkins described the Army agreement as a "wonderful badge of seriousness," and Tanjga highlighted consistent execution and strategic investments. In Q&A, management maintained a steady and detailed tone, addressing questions directly.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management conveyed greater confidence, with less discussion of macro uncertainty or conservatism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance language was more optimistic, with management highlighting sustained growth and the initiation of a capital return policy. The previous quarter referenced macro uncertainty and government shutdown disruptions.
  • Strategic focus shifted to scaling AI-driven revenue and expanding high-value enterprise deals, with the Army agreement marking a new milestone.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on AI, public sector deals, and sales productivity, but the current quarter included more forward-looking questions about resource allocation and sales capacity growth.
  • Key metrics improved: cloud net ARR expansion rose to 114% from 112%, and $1 million ARR customers increased from 115 to 140. Management's tone indicated increased confidence in both profitability and pipeline.
  • The company introduced new financial reporting metrics and announced a $50 million stock buyback, signaling greater financial flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged investor concerns about AI as an existential threat to software firms, but Calkins emphasized Appian's positioning as essential to AI workflows.
  • FX impacts were noted as a factor in growth rates, with normalization expected after Q1 2026.
  • Tanjga highlighted a return to moderate investment pace in OpEx and the need for responsible scaling of sales and engineering capacity.
  • No new material business risks were raised by analysts.

Final Takeaway

Appian's Q4 2025 call demonstrated robust growth in cloud and total revenue, highlighted by a 50% rise in large enterprise deals and a major $500 million Army contract. The company guided for 16% cloud revenue growth for 2026, introduced new financial metrics, and announced a $50 million share buyback. Management emphasized the synergy between AI and process orchestration, with customer upgrades to AI tiers driving revenue and pipeline expansion. Analysts responded positively to the company's momentum, and management projected continued confidence and operational discipline as Appian enters a new phase of capital return and strategic investment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The U.S. trade deficit in 2025 stands at $901B
data.description:
The U.S. trade deficit for 2025 came in at $901.5 billion, down only 0.2%, or by $2.1 billion, in a year that saw the Trump administration announce a slew of tariffs. 

The U.S. international trade in goods and services deficit increased 32.6% to $70.3B in December vs. $55.50B consensus and $53.00B in November (revised from $56.8B), according to data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Thursday.

The data comes after President Donald Trump announced a slew of aggressive tariffs, which looked to bring the deficit down. The president had looked to implement a series of reciprocal tariffs on certain countries that had surpluses against the U.S. 

Over the year, however, Trump also did roll back some tariffs, and talks were still ongoing with many countries to reach more trade deals. 

Exports were $3.43 trillion, up $199.8 billion from 2024, and imports were $4.3 trillion, up $197.8 billion from the year prior. 

More on the U.S. Economy

  • Some Fed members appear open to rate hike if inflation remains above target: FOMC minutes
  • Durable goods orders fall less than expected in December
  • Economic Data Alphabet Soup On The Menu: FOMC Minutes, GDP, PCE, PMIs, UMich

data.title: Sandfire Resources reports 1H 2026 results
data.description:
• Sandfire Resources press release (SFRRF): 1H 2026
  • Sales revenue of $672M (+17.5% Y/Y) beats by $1.78M.
  • Profit after tax $96M.
